metadata.title: The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science: An In-Depth Literature Review

abstract: In the past decade, applying machine learning methods in political science has grown in popularity. The increase in data volume and sources motivated researchers to turn to these data-driven methods as an alternative to classical statistics. Several review papers have proposed theoretical typologies for applying machine learning in social sciences. We present an overview of how and why machine learning methods are actually implemented in the field of political science. The aim of this study is to conduct an empirical analysis of the political science literature that uses machine learning as a research method. We applied the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses) framework for systematic review studies to systematically select 339 articles from Web of Science and Scopus, evaluated their relevance based on a set of inclusion criteria, and created a database with the key characteristics. Overall, we observed that political scientists have embraced machine learning as empirical method based on the increased use in the past 10 years. We found that the use of machine learning is the most prevalent in political communication and conflict and peace studies, and that topic modeling, support vector machine, and random forest are the most used methods. Our results indicate that reporting on optimizing machine learning models through hyperparameter tuning could be more transparent, and researchers should conduct their own benchmarking when choosing the most suitable model.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: In social science, classical statistics have been used for decades as the fundamental toolbox to discover (causal) relationships and generate insights regarding a multitude of phenomena. The immense surge in generated data, different data types, and data sources in the past decade obliged scientists from various disciplines to turn to more advanced data platforms for managing and analyzing these data. As a consequence, there has also been a turn to machine learning methods, which allow for the analysis of large data volumes as well as for the unraveling of the complex relationships between phenomena (Cranmer, 2019;Grimmer et al., 2021;Radford and Joseph, 2020).

Machine learning, however, originated in computer science, which traditionally focused on the development of algorithms and in the majority of cases, on predictive models (Hofman et al., 2021;Shmueli, 2010). This ambition may be perceived as incongruent with the predilection within certain realms of political science for understanding relationships, explaining phenomena, or examining causal mechanisms that are based on theory. Nevertheless, recent scholarship suggests that this perceived tension between prediction and explanation may be surmountable, as political scientists, too, have increasingly started to use machine learning methods, and data scientists also use machine learning to examine causal mechanisms. Indeed, because of the increased need to analyze large text corpora, the mass use of social media for communication purposes, and the increased incorporation of algorithms in daily political life itself, political scientists increasingly started to embrace these methods in their respective research domains, e.g. (Beltran et al., 2020;Blair and Sambanis, 2020;Greene and Cross, 2017).

Yet, there has been limited research on how the adoption of machine learning affects the research practices of political science, nor on the practical applications of machine learning within the field of political science. Several scholars have provided crucial typologies to start to structure this debate about the nexus between machine learning methods and political science. Athey and Imbens (2019) and Molina and Garip (2019) elaborate on the applicability of supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement machine learning methods, thus giving an important stepping stone for understanding how machine learning methods may be applied in the social sciences. Also, Hofman et al. (2021) describe the different empirical modeling paradigms, and their potential relevance for political science. In addition to the more prevalent modeling types (i.e., descriptive, explanatory, and predictive), they propose integrative modeling as a combination of explanatory and predictive capabilities, to predict outcomes and estimate effects in as yet unseen situations. Furthermore, in the broader social science, various authors have already elaborated on an (discipline-agnostic) approach to which machine learning in particular can be applied for a research task (Grimmer et al., 2021). What needs to be added, however, is an empirical analysis of how and why machine learning methods are currently used in practice in political science.

Such an analysis is crucial, especially when considering the suddenness with which machine learning pushed itself into various academic disciplines. The current hype surrounding machine learning and the limited time researchers have had to familiarize themselves with these vastly complex methods raises important questions. On the one hand, are methods sometimes applied in ways that result in sub-optimal or erroneous outcomes as confusion might exist about what a specific method can accomplish? On the other hand, do new machine learning methods actually provide added value in comparison to more traditional methods from statistics? In this sense, machine learning is no different from other methodological innovations that shaped the domain of political science in the past. There have been trends in the use of certain novel methods for empirical research, and it is important to scrutinize whether a new method is used merely because it is part of an emerging trend or because it has consistently proven itself as a useful and appropriate tool. Similarly, it is important to wonder what happens to epistemological tensions and baselines when methods travel across disciplines and what their migration to another domain looks like.
It is to these debates that this article speaks through the following operationalizable research questions, which were examined based on a systematic literature review:

1. In which research domains are machine learning methods most commonly utilized? We analyzed which political research domains are more mature in leveraging machine learning methods. This is tied to the type of data that is under analysis. 2. Which machine learning methods are the most and least prevalent in political science? We investigate which type of research questions and data are typically analyzed using machine learning and which methods are mostly used by political scientists. 3. Are these machine learning methods implemented according to best practices in data science? We evaluate how political scientists approach the design of the machine learning method used in their empirical data analysis. 4. What are the opportunities for future research given the rapid evolution in machine learning? We assess how machine learning methods are matched with the research task and whether the state-of-the-art method has been chosen.

In spite of political scientists' growing interest in machine learning methods, there is currently no encompassing state-of-the-art overview regarding how machine learning is currently applied in the field of political science. As such, these questions related to how machine learning methods are currently used in the field of political science and how this has evolved over time, have so far not been systematically addressed in the literature.

In the remainder of this paper, we first elaborate on the contextualization and the review method. Then we present the results and conclude the article with a set of recommendations on how to expand the adoption of machine learning as well as on new research paths where machine learning can generate additional insights to the political science community.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: Researching the literature, there are various definitions for machine learning presented (Hong et al., 2018;Sebastiani, 2002;Zhang and Zhou, 2014).

While it is not the aim of this article to elaborate on each type and method of machine learning (for elaboration, see Grimmer et al., 2021;Jordan and Mitchell, 2015;Molina and Garip, 2019), this overview is needed to subsequently discuss the nexus between these machine learning paradigms and political science.

Historically, "machine learning" has been defined as "the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without explicitly being programmed" (Samuel, 1959: 211). As machine learning evolved and its complexity rose, however, this general definition became increasingly inadequate and obsolete, and today, scholars typically differentiate between three paradigms of machine learning methods: supervised machine learning, unsupervised machine learning, and reinforcement learning (Cranmer, 2019;Jordan and Mitchell, 2015;Molina and Garip, 2019). Supervised machine learning (SML) is the most prevalent paradigm of machine learning, where the aim is to train a model given a set of inputs and labels, which is used to make predictions, and where the performance is measured on out-of-sample data. Here, we would like to stress the distinction between prediction in the formal sense and forecasting. While prediction is considered to estimate the outcome based on out-of-sample feature values, forecasting is considered to predict future events (Jordan and Mitchell, 2015). Here, classification problems concern discrete data, and regression problems concern continuous data. In some articles, this definition focusing on making data-driven predictions is the definition given to machine learning (Gründler and Krieger, 2016;Ramey et al., 2016;Rheault and Cochrane, 2019).

Unsupervised machine learning (UML), on the contrary, has the objective to unravel the underlying structure in unlabeled data (Jordan and Mitchell, 2015). It essentially searches for a representation of the input that is more understandable than the input itself. Here, we see dimension reduction techniques and clustering as the main categories. Semi-SML can be considered a blend of supervised and unsupervised learning, where unlabeled data or auxiliary information is used to augment labeled data in a supervised learning context.

The third paradigm is reinforcement learning. The aim is to have an agent find a set of actions that optimizes a certain objective based on the data that is generated while the agent interacts with the environment. As it is assumed that rewards are provided after each action, reinforcement learning problems have the objective of maximizing the expected reward over time when learning a policy for an agent in an initially unknown environment where that policy is trained to select the right actions for each state (Jordan and Mitchell, 2015). In simpler terms, reinforcement learning learns how to maximize the output through trial and error.

One of the advantages of using machine learning methods for predictions is that fewer assumptions need to be made on the distribution of the variables and that it is possible to automatically detect non-linear relations and insights on the structure from big data sources. For these benefits to materialize, it is crucial that these methods are adequately selected, applied, and interpreted. Today, machine learning packages are available that are capable of gaining accurate insights from the data, yet bad model generalization for predictions, unjustified interpretations or unwanted bias in practice sometimes challenge the results coming out of these models (Molnar et al., 2022;van Giffen et al., 2022). One specific concern in this regard is the selection of the most suitable machine learning method for a specific research objective. This requires carefully tailoring the machine learning method based on the structure of the data and meticulous hyperparameter tuning. Hyperparameter tuning is a systematic search of the most optimal set of hyperparameters of a machine learning model. Hyper-parameters exist in parallel of the model parameters; they control the process of how a model is learning based on the data and the performance on out-of-sample data, without actually being part of the model itself (Arnold et al., 2024).

Another attention point is that while machine learning is a celebrated tool in detecting associations between variables, one cannot derive causal relationships from the data in a straightforward way. This brings us back to the question of whether there might be a tension between machine learning methods and the predilection of significant parts of the domain of political science for making causal inference based on empirical analysis (Breiman, 2001b). However, recent trends in modern causality in machine learning specifically focus on this with the aim of causal discovery and causal effect estimation (Nogueira et al., 2022).

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: General Approach. For this review, we have executed a systematic literature search in the Web of Science and Scopus databases for peer-reviewed articles with a cut-off date of December 31, 2022. The review study is based on the guidelines provided by the PRISMA 2020 (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses) statement, a framework to guide the creation of systematic reviews in a structured manner (Page et al., 2021). The review protocol has been pre-registered on the Open Science Framework (OSF) before initiating the review process. The search queries, list of eligible journals, and bibliographical analysis can also be consulted on OSF (https://osf.io/56b4r/?view_only=8 6e93ea2b6dc458cb154c8203ac0f36b). As broad criteria, we included studies that used machine learning methods in their empirical analysis. Furthermore, we executed a set of queries to search articles that use specific machine learning methods or method classes.

In Web of Science, we searched using the queries as mentioned in Appendix 1. Search Queries (see https://osf.io/56b4r/?view_only=86e93ea2b6dc458cb154c8203ac0f36b). To illustrate, we provide a set of example queries: Topic = "Machine learning," Topic = "Random Forest," Topic = "Neural Networks" OR "Neural Nets." We ensured that the returned items were in the domain of political science and therefore refined by categories "Political Science," "Public Administration," "International Relations," "Social Sciences," "Interdisciplinary," "Social Issues," "Multidisciplinary Sciences" and "Social Sciences Mathematical Methods." In Scopus, we used the same keywords, limiting the sub-area to "SOCI," the source type to journal and the language to English.

We removed all duplicates from both sources based on DOI number. To create a shortlist of articles for review, we conducted a double-blind abstract evaluation to determine their relevance, considering the inclusion criteria. This abstract evaluation was facilitated using the online tool abstrackr™ (Wallace et al., 2012), and with two reviewers, we obtained an intercoder reliability of 79%, which indicates substantial agreement (McHugh, 2012). In case the two reviewers had a disagreement on the evaluation of the abstract, a discussion followed to obtain a consensus on whether to include the article in the review study. We conducted a full-text evaluation of this shortlist of articles and assessed the eligibility of incorporating these articles in the review (Page et al., 2021). In the first stage, we identified the primary research discipline(s) of the author(s) by viewing the biographies mentioned in the articles, the mentioned affiliation with a certain department or faculty, or by a web search. We categorized them as political or social scientists if all authors are introduced as political scientists or are affiliated with a political and social science research department. Furthermore, we defined a category for computer scientists if the authors are described as computer scientists or are affiliated with a research department in mathematics or computer science. We also added a category statisticians or data scientists if the authors are described as statisticians or data scientists, are affiliated with a research department in statistics or data science, or if they are aligned with a Quantitative Methods research department inside a political and social science faculty. With interdisciplinary team we understood the combination of political or social scientists with either computer scientists or statisticians/data scientists. Finally, the category other is for all other affiliations, such as environmental scientists and psychologists. Furthermore, we recorded which political research domain the articles can be categorized into, for example, political communication and public policy. We are interested in the prevalence of machine learning methods in different fields since different domains of society have become more algorithm-driven, and political scientists are more interested in this phenomenon. We registered the method or method class together with the most important details or description that are mentioned in the article. In the last stage, we recorded the aim of the specific modeling: descriptive, explanatory, or predictive. Especially when explanatory modeling was used, we verified whether it was to detect the causal relationships between variables and what measures did the authors take to unwrap the black box of the machine learning method and to gain insights on the contribution of the variables to the output.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: For both the abstract evaluation and the full-text evaluation, we applied a set of inclusion and exclusion criteria to ensure that the most relevant articles were selected. We considered only empirical articles written in English where machine learning methods were used for the data analysis, where the full text was available and which were published in peer-reviewed journals categorized as "political science" according to the Journal Citations Reports 2022. The full list of 295 journals can be consulted in the Appendix 2. Eligible Journals (see https://osf. io/56b4r/?view_only=86e93ea2b6dc458cb154c8203ac0f36b). We excluded studies that describe machine learning as a phenomenon through a political science lens or where machine learning is the topic under research, rather than the method applied.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: Concretely, the queries executed in Web of Science and Scopus resulted in respectively a total of 60.561 and 67.494 articles. After filtering the journal and removing duplicates, we obtained 775 resp. 101 articles. The double-blind evaluation based on title, abstract, and keywords scan generated 460 resp. 65 articles as shortlisted. In total, 351 articles were filtered out during this phase, as they were deemed not relevant for the review based on the topic or the applied method. As a final step, a full-text scan was performed resulting in retaining 339 articles to be included in the review. During this step, 14 articles were not retained because the main text was not written in English and 172 articles were filtered out as they were deemed not relevant: the study was not conducted in the political science domain or the employed method was not considered as a machine learning method.

Regarding the latter criterion, it is noteworthy that cluster analysis was the most prevalent method in the initial sample, however these articles have been excluded from our final sample because the majority of these articles (75.2%) utilized standard cluster analysis methods (e.g., hierarchical cluster analysis, K-means) on a rather limited dataset, which should not be considered machine learning per se: this method has been a popular statistical technique for over half a century (Jain et al., 1999), for example, in political science to study comparative politics and political behavior. Although some machine learning scholars consider cluster analysis to be a machine learning technique, for the purpose of this article, the decision was made to only incorporate articles using advanced clustering algorithms tailored to large data sets and requiring significant computing power, or if it was explicitly mentioned in the article that it is a machine learning technique. As a summary, the filtering process is visualized in Figure 1.

sections.header_raw: Evolution of Articles Using Machine Learning or Artificial Intelligence
sections.header_norm: evolution of articles using machine learning or artificial intelligence
paragraphs: From our analysis, it can be observed that in most areas of political science, a preference continues to exist for utilizing deductive methods as researchers seek for explanations that are intuitively and interpretatively describing certain phenomena and rely on foundational theory to derive causal mechanisms. The initial reluctance to introduce inductive methods was because of the finality of the latter methods: generating predictions and uncover associations between outcomes and features (Breiman, 2001b).

Our analysis of the 339 published articles in peer-reviewed journals that use machine learning models (see Appendix 3. Bibliography Analysis) shows that there has been a steady increase in number of articles using machine learning models in political science starting from 2013 and in 2017 there has been a steep increase with a peak of 90 publications in 2022. Figure 2 visualizes the number of published articles per year, starting from the earliest publication in our sample from 1990 till the latest one in 2022. What is also striking is the article from 1990 followed by a gap of 7 years (Schrodt, 1990). The article utilizes the ID3 algorithm, the predecessor of the more prevalent C4.5 algorithm, to draw decision trees on a rather limited sample size (n = 310)

paragraphs: In Table 2 the top 10 journals with the most published articles using machine learning methods are displayed with the fraction of articles they represent of the total. Political Analysis has published the highest number of articles using machine learning as the primary empirical data analysis tool (43 out of 339; 12.7%). This is in line with the journal's focus on methodology, the theorization of novel methods, and their applicability to the political science domain. American Political Science Review, Journal of Information Technology & Politics, and Journal of Peace Research with each 13 out of 339 (3.8%) publications have also been among the front-runners of publishing machine learningdriven political science research as well as Political Communication with 12 out of 339 publications (3.5%). It illustrates that, although on the rise, machine learning methods are used rather limited in a broad range of journals.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: Political scientists have long struggled to analyze texts (discourse and sentiment analysis) and deduce any insights due to the vast amount of political texts. Traditionally, it takes an enormous amount of funds to manually read all texts and code the features researchers are interested in. However, with the up-rise of automated text mining and content analysis techniques, researchers were presented with the possibility to draw inferences in politics based on large corpora (Grimmer and Stewart, 2013). Our analysis shows that the majority of articles using machine learning in the domain of political science apply these methods on text-as-data. As visualized in Figure 3, 68.4% of the articles analyze text-as-data. 29.2% of the articles under analysis applied machine learning methods to tabular data (majority is survey data). Only 2.4% of the articles treat images-as-data or applied computer vision methods.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: For a more comprehensive understanding of the research purpose of the different articles, we categorized the modeling types as descriptive, predictive, or explanatory.

As displayed in Figure 4, 61.9% of the articles were using machine learning methods for predictive purposes. In Piscopo et al. (2017), for example, researchers analyzed what dimensions contribute to the sense of community and participation using random forests on US census data. Furthermore, we observe that 35.7% of the articles use descriptive modeling tools. The study by Kaneko et al. (2021) proposes a new approach to estimating the ideal points of media outlets, which are estimated by applying latent topic modeling to newspaper editorials.

Only 2.4% of the total number of articles mention techniques for explanatory modeling, where causal relationships are drawn between variables or estimating certain treatment effects. Yin et al. (2021), for example, use causal forest to examine the effect of state coercion on citizens' political attitudes. This was done by focusing on how repressive measures affected the voting outcome in the 2019 District Council Election in Hong Kong. An additional analysis showed that only 7.4% of the total number of articles are looking more in-depth into the black box of the machine learning algorithm, looking into the explainability of the output given the set of features. The difference with explanatory modeling techniques is that in most cases a post hoc analysis is required to explain what contributes to the outcome of the method, such as Variable Importance Plots, Partial Dependence Plots, and Individual Conditional Expectation (Breiman, 2001a;Cafri and Bailey, 2022;Goldstein et al., 2015).

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
sections.header_raw: Author Composition and the Correlation with Reproducibility Measures
sections.header_norm: author composition and the correlation with reproducibility measures
paragraphs: When we examine the author composition, we observe that author or author teams consisting of only social/political scientists are the most prevalent ones. The division is visualized in Figure 5. We considered five groups: Political or social scientists, authors with a statistics or data science background, computer scientists, interdisciplinary teams, or other. 73.7% of the analyzed articles are authored by social scientists. Only 19.8% of the articles are written by an alliance of social scientists and data scientists/mathematicians/ computer scientists, and we notice that such alliance coalitions have increased in prevalence since 2016. This overrepresentation of political or social scientists makes sense, given our selection of journals, but it is also striking, given how much expertise exists in other disciplines, which is seemingly not tapped into.

We furthermore analyzed the relation between the author's composition and the reproducibility measures that were taken. We registered for every article in the sample, whether (1) the data, (2) the code, and (3) the seeds were documented in the appendix, code repository, or Dataverse. We hypothesized that interdisciplinary teams including a data scientist/mathematician/computer scientist would take the best practices regarding reproducibility measures in machine learning into account more consistently. We assumed that these best practices were determined by the original creators or developers of the different machine learning methods and that mastering these specific best practices is mostly done by the researchers that have machine learning at the core of their research and, in the second place researchers from other domains that use these machine learning methods as tools for their research. Table 3 visualizes the percentage of publications from political/ social scientists and interdisciplinary teams that made the data set, the code, or the used random seeds available. It is apparent that in the three categories, a higher percentage of teams with solely political/social scientists report their data set, code, or random seed than the interdisciplinary teams. 40.4% of teams with solely political/social scientists make the examined data set public, 37.6% the code and 22.8% the random seeds compared to 31.3%, 28.4% and 11.9% of the interdisciplinary teams respectively. This means that we can reject our initial hypothesis.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: To derive an overview of the different research domains to which the machine learning techniques were applied, we attached to each article one or more domains and determined what the most popular research domains are. Table 4 displays the top 10 most frequent research domains in political science that were examined in our sample of articles, with the number of articles and the percentage of the total (n = 339). We observe that 25.4% of the articles in our sample are in the domain of political communication. This is in line with the fact that the majority of articles in our sample researches text-as-data (68.4%). We see specifically corpora of speeches from politicians, press releases, transcripts of hearings, manifestos, and most importantly, social media traffic. 15.9% of the articles are in conflict and peace studies, as there is significant research on tabular data and geolocation data to predict certain events, such as civil wars or other conflicts. Finally, 14.7% of articles are in policy studies, which is explained by the numerous topic modeling/text mining techniques that are conducted on policy texts.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
sections.header_raw: Machine Learning Methods and How They Are Applied
sections.header_norm: machine learning methods and how they are applied
paragraphs: We analyzed the number of instances in which a certain machine learning method is utilized in our sample. In some cases, multiple methods are used to benchmark the results.

Table 5 provides a ranked overview of most frequently used machine learning methods, what paradigm they follow, and the number and percentage of articles it was used in. A first striking finding is that in none of the articles, reinforcement learning was applied.

The most prevalent technique, on the contrary, was topic modeling (24.8%). In the majority of cases structural topic modeling for analyzing text (in 31 articles) and latent Dirichlet allocation (in 22 articles) were applied.

In the cases where tabular data is analyzed, random forest and support vector machine (SVM) are both the most popular methods for predictions; 16.2% of the articles use the random forest and 16.2% of the articles use SVM. In the latter case, this is mainly because SVM is referred to in the articles as more performant in general when benchmarking with other models (Diermeier et al., 2012;Dumais et al., 1998).

Although logistic regression could be considered as a classical statistics method, articles were included in our sample when it was explicitly mentioned that they were considered as a machine learning method and when the method was used for prediction with performance metrics on out-of-sample data.

In addition, the table contains mostly method classes, where several specific methods are aggregated by. Only random forest and SVM are methods that are explicitly mentioned due to their frequent use. For topic modeling, we mostly use structured topic modeling and latent Dirichlet allocation. Under Bayesian machine learning, we find mostly bayesian additive regression trees (BART) and Bayesian regression, and with regularization methods, we understand lasso, ridge, and elastic net regression. Under other treebased methods, we include gradient-boosted trees, adaboosted trees, and decision trees; with other text mining methods, we group all specific methods to analyze texts that do not fall under topic modeling or natural language processing. Finally, the table also contains values for SML and Semi-supervised Machine Learning since, in seven articles, the exact machine learning method was not mentioned nor described.

To add to these results, we analyzed the fraction of articles where different methods for predictive purposes were compared to each other. Specifically, we tried to understand whether the author was able to shortlist a set of machine learning methods, optimize the models, and select the most suitable model for the empirical analysis after a benchmark.

Of the 210 articles that used machine learning methods for predictive purposes, 48 (22.9%) articles mentioned that a benchmarking exercise was performed. In the majority of the articles, the authors selected a method based on their literature study and in which another setup, a certain method is performing best. Hyperparameter tuning is considered as a crucial step in the model design and training phase to maximize the performance of the used machine learning model. We analyzed whether there is a link between reporting the hyperparameter tuning measures in the article published in a journal and the quartile in which this journal is ranked (considered as a quality label of a journal). Table 6 shows the percentage of articles that report on the use of hyperparameter tuning grouped per quartile of the journals it is published in. We observe that articles from Q1 journals report marginally more on the used hyperparameter tuning measures compared to articles from Q2 (χ 2 = 2.60, p = 0.1066) and significantly more compared to Q3 or Q4 journals (χ 2 = 4.48, p = 0.0343). However, in Q1 journals still a minority of articles report on the hyperparameter tuning measures.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: Machine learning methods are increasingly popular within the field of political science. Although several review papers have offered theoretical frameworks for applying machine learning in political science and the broader social sciences (Grimmer et al., 2021;Molina and Garip, 2019), what remains absent is a comprehensive overview of the practical implementation and rationale behind the utilization of machine learning methods within the field of political science. Against that background, this review article presents an exhaustive overview of how and why machine learning methods are currently implemented in the field of political science. We applied the PRISMA framework for systematic review studies and identified and analyzed 339 articles published in the field of political science between 1990 and 2022, during which the empirical data analysis was conducted using machine learning methods.

Our results show that the use of machine learning methods has risen, looking at the number of publications per year. This growing reliance can be explained by the fact that technical innovation has enabled political scientists, like scientists across the board, to store and immediately access numerous amounts of text or other kinds of data (Siddiqa et al., 2017). Furthermore, because of continuous improvement and development of machine learning methods in different domains, political scientists will have the opportunity to adopt machine learning more frequently, should a suitable research question appear. Below, we present the five key findings and associated opportunities and recommendations for further research that our review revealed.

First, text-as-data is the most prevalent data type (68.4%). This can be explained by the availability of big data platforms and methods to store and analyze large text corpora, such as speeches, manifestos, and web content. Another reason for the sudden and sharp increase in available data is the impressive rise of social media, which has been used as a new political communication channel in the past decennium. The impact social media messaging evokes on public behavior and public opinion stimulated scholars to research this new phenomenon, and machine learning has played an important role in mining millions of social media posts. In contrast, image-as-data was rarely (2.4% of articles) analyzed in our sample. This is surprising since images are an important vehicle to extend a message to the target audience. Research in political communication still focuses on text and text-based methods, while there is a lack of engagement with visual methods (Farkas and Bene, 2021). In our sample of articles, 68 articles conducted research on social media posts, but only two of them analyzed images. Especially given current trends in (social) media consumption and the personalization of political communication, more engagement with image-as-data constitutes a potentially promising avenue for the application of machine learning in political science, for example, computer vision for facial detection and recognition, sentiment analysis, and how it enforces the textual message (Farkas and Bene, 2021).

Second, in the majority of articles (61.9%), machine learning methods were applied for prediction purposes. This partially indicates that political scientists have embraced the potential of these novel research tools and that predictive research is taking up a more important place in the political science domain alongside, among other things, explanatory and descriptive research. However, only 2.4% of the articles used machine learning techniques, for example, causal forest, for explanatory purposes. The next striking observation relates to explainability; only 7.4% of the total number of articles applied tools to explain the predicted output values based on the input features. One of the reasons is that when machine learning is used in this case, it is mainly focused on the predictions itself and the performance rather than explanation. Here, there is still untapped potential of leveraging these tools to add an explainability dimension to the empirical research. One avenue proposed by Hofman et al. (2021) is the approach of integrative modeling, which is a combination of explanatory and predictive modeling types to predict outcomes and estimate effects in as-yet-unseen situations. In addition, explainability measures could be applied as well to unravel the used black box machine learning models. First, visualization techniques could already provide first indication, such as Partial Dependence Plots and Individual Conditional Expectation, are recommended due to their modelagnosticity and intuitively interpretable output (Ribeiro et al., 2016). Second, one can use model-based techniques for explainability, such as SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), whose objective is to explain the prediction of an instance by assigning an importance value to each feature in a model (Lundberg and Lee, 2017). Features with positive/negative SHAP values positively/negatively impact the prediction, while the magnitude is a measure of how strong the effect is. Choi et al. (2022), from our sample, used SHAP to understand how certain factors drive structuring public support for a variety of climate change mitigation policies. Another popular method, but nowhere exploited in our sample, is Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations. Here, local surrogate models are derived separately from the machine learning models and are trained to explain individual predictions, resulting in a simpler interpretable model (Ribeiro et al., 2016). Besides model-agnostic methods, there are also model-specific method, for example, TreeSHAP, for analyzing tree-based machine learning models as proposed by Lundberg and Lee (2017). Or Expected Gradients and Integrated Gradients techniques for neural networks, as discussed by Erion et al. (2021) and Madsen et al. (2022). As political decisions and policies have broad-reaching implications for society, it is crucial that models informing these decisions are transparent and interpretable. This ensures that derived predictions and insights are not only accurate but also understandable, allowing for informed decision-making.

The third pertinent observation relates to the author composition of the articles in our sample, and we observed that the majority (73.7%) of the articles are written by a social/ political scientist or a team of social/political scientists, which is unsurprising given the list of journals we selected from. What is striking, though, is that in spite of the complexity and novelty of these methods, which form an entire field of study in and of themselves, only a minority (19.2%) of the articles were written by a consortium of social/political scientists and machine learning experts. While one may assume that interdisciplinary teams including at least one machine learning expert would adhere more strongly to best practices on reproducibility when using machine learning than teams consisting of only social/political scientists, our analysis showed that this was not the case. We observed that teams consisting of only social/political scientists were more compliant with reproducibility best practices when providing access to the code base and data set. We limited our analysis to computational reproducibility of the results, that is, making exact code and data available. Kapoor and Narayanan (2023) considered additional criteria that the data analysis has to be correctly conducted. They defined eight cases of data leakage, ranging from same data set for training and testing to sampling bias in test distribution, that should be prevented in order for the results to be genuinely reproducible. In (Kapoor et al., 2023), the authors went a step further and proposed a reporting standard that focuses on top of reproducibility, validity, and Generalizability, by creating a checklist consisting of 32 questions for ML-based science that authors may use for additional reporting in their publications. At the same time, this is only one dimension of quality control, and involving machine learning experts may still offer crucial benefits in terms of contributing to the design of the considered methods, raising awareness about important new developments, or guiding on programming more advanced methods. This is also related to the next fourth observation: the use of more advanced methods is rather limited. Researchers mostly rely on standard and proven methods. Topic modeling is the most prevalent method for text-as-data in our sample of articles. More specifically, structural topic modeling was the most used topic model to discover the hidden thematic structure in large text corpora. For articles in our sample that investigated tabular data, both random forest and SVM were the most applied machine learning methods. The performance they generally demonstrate, and the relative ease of implementation are likely the reasons why these methods are frequently used.

While we do not argue in favor or against a certain method in this article (for a detailed elaboration, see Grimmer et al., 2021;Molina and Garip, 2019), we advise careful attention to the process of and decision criteria for selecting a certain method, as we have observed that only 22.9% of the articles in our sample, that uses machine learning for predictions, mentioned that a benchmarking exercise was performed before the most suitable method was selected for the conducted research. This implies that in the majority of the articles, the authors selected a method based on their literature study. They read about a method and the performance it showed in a certain research context and decided to apply the same method to their own research. The risk may arise when transferring a certain method to another context, such that the optimal performance in the previous situation cannot be guaranteed. This is related to the no-free-lunch theorem, which postulates that "there is no formal ground for claiming that some machine learning algorithms are performing better than others in reaching goals that we have conceptualize them to reach" (Sterkenburg and Grunwald, 2021: 9980). As such, there is an argument to make for researchers performing their own benchmark based on the research context and tailored to the analyzed data. Benchmarking provides an opportunity to design, tune, and maximize the performance of every method in the benchmark. In the end, based on a set of performance metrics, the most suitable model, or an ensemble of the benchmarked models may be selected for predictions. In this regard, the potential added value of composing interdisciplinary teams that involve a machine learning expert seems evident in helping to create and select the optimal model. Due to the fast-emerging field of machine learning, more specialization is required, and a more intense collaboration between political scientists and machine learning experts can help to gain the most from each field. In the other direction, machine learning scientists may benefit from involving political scientists in their research to validate their newly created methods and translate them to other empirical domains.

When conducting a benchmarking exercise, hyperparameter tuning is an important procedure. Regarding applying hyperparameter tuning measures to optimize machine learning models, we observed that its use has been reported in only a minority of cases (overall 41.6%). Arnold et al. (2024) are taking it a step further in analyzing the use and reporting on hyperparameter tuning. They postulate that hyperparameter tuning is critical in maximizing the performance of the model and that reporting on hyperparameters is required for scientific progress and guarantees that the conducted research is reproducible. They analyzed a sample of 64 articles from three leading political science journals and registered whether (1) tunable hyper-parameters were involved in the models; (2) the final hyperparameter values of the models were mentioned in the article; and (3) the hyperparameter search method was described in the paper. Only in 20.3% of the articles from their sample had added the details regarding the hyperparameter search and selection leading to optimal performance, which is in line with our findings (Arnold et al., 2024).

sections.header_norm: limitations of the conducted review study
paragraphs: While the previous paragraphs zoomed in on a number of overarching insights, there are important limitations regarding our research. First, the sample of articles that we have reviewed is limited due to the inclusion criteria. We only included peer-reviewed journal articles, while conference papers could have been potentially relevant to be reviewed as well. Furthermore, we filtered the 295 journals that appeared in the Journal Citations Reports. We might have overlooked articles from political science journals outside this list but could have been relevant, we might have excluded research from political scientists that published machine learning-related work outside of political science journals, or we might have excluded articles from journals in machine learning that have an empirical case study in political science.

In addition, we filtered on broad terms such as "machine learning" but also on commonly used machine learning methods. Potentially, articles using a novel or niche machine learning technique were therefore not included in our sample. Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have boosted the research in AI (Ouyang et al., 2022). However, the impact of LLMs on political sciences research lies beyond the scope of the current analysis. Therefore, articles that use LLMs for empirical analysis were not incorporated into our sample. At this point, LLMs are not frequently used in political science, but it could be used as a tool for sentiment analysis, tone of voice detection in political texts, or evaluating text paragraphs on an ideological scale (Ornstein et al., 2023).

Finally, we only relied on information that was shared in the articles and its appendices, Dataverse, or other online repositories, to code the articles in our database. We realize that the whole procedure of the research or applying the machine learning technique is not reported in detail. Nevertheless, we are confident that the systematic approach that was followed was rigorous and enabled us to analyze a broad sample of articles, leading to a range of solid conclusions and recommendations.

sections.paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review
paragraphs: We observed through this literature review an increase in academic articles in political science that apply machine learning for empirical analysis, indicating that political scientists have started to embrace these data-driven methods. Nonetheless, this study underscores that there are still underexplored research directions and additional dimensions for consideration in future scholarly work. We hope that this review study will strengthen the bridge between political science and machine learning, where best practices from the machine learning domain will be transported and newly formulated research questions in political science will inspire researchers to apply more advanced methods (e.g., LLMs) and to develop novel machine learning techniques, that are relevant and tailored to the research context.
